content: Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is a transient episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal brain, spinal cord, or retinal ischemia without acute infarction. Traditional definition: symptoms <24 hours; tissue-based definition: no infarction on MRI DWI. TIA is a medical emergency—stroke risk is 10-15% in first 90 days, with ~50% occurring within 48 hours ('warning stroke'). Typical symptoms: sudden onset focal deficit (unilateral weakness/numbness, aphasia, dysarthria, visual field defect, ataxia); negative symptoms (loss of function) rather than positive (spreading tingling suggests migraine). Amaurosis fugax: transient monocular vision loss from ipsilateral carotid emboli ('curtain descending'). Duration typically <1 hour (median 10 minutes). ABCD2 score predicts early stroke risk: Age ≥60 (1), BP ≥140/90 (1), Clinical features (unilateral weakness 2, speech impairment without weakness 1), Duration ≥60 min (1), Diabetes (1). Score 0-3 low risk, 4-5 moderate, 6-7 high risk.
